sentences.text: Adam Melcer, A-D-A-M M-E-L-C-E-R.
Same name.
Except Adam, I had Abraham.
I was named after my mother's father.
My birthday is December 8, 1913.
Well, I share a birthday with my wife and my son.
Warsaw, Poland.
In Warsaw.
Yes, my father is Menachim, was a carpenter.
He was born in the Wlodawa.
Warsaw.
He passed on in 1936.
He had heart failure.
Average guy.
He worked very hard.
And that's all.
I guess.
My mother was a housewife.
Esther Faiga.
Maiden name, yes.
It was Rotbaum.
About the same, '85-- 1885.
She passed away in 1942 in the Warsaw, when the Germans took her away in Warsaw.
In 1942, it was a-- that time the Germans collected all the Jews in Warsaw for-- and they said they're going to camps.
But as far as we know, she went to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a camp, and they killed her.
She was a very nice person.
She had four children, and she was a-- a housewife.
Yes.
Yes, I had a older sister, Sarah, Polish name was [?
Sala. ?] And she was all the time with us in Warsaw.
And then they-- after the uprising, they took her also away.
And she was in Majdanek, as far as I know.
And she was killed in Majdanek--
--in 19-- she was born in 1911.
She died in 1944.
Maybe the end of '43.
It was after the uprising in Warsaw.
She was taken out from Warsaw and to Majdanek.
She was a-- a nice working girl.
And she was working when she go up in some stores, a saleslady in Warsaw.
She got married in the Warsaw Ghetto.
No.
I don't remember.
My other sister is-- was two years younger than I am, and her name was Genia.
And she was taken away during the Yom Kippur action in Warsaw.
And she probably went to Treblinka also.
And then, never heard of her.
She also, she was a younger girl at that time.
And she went to school, and at that time in Warsaw, she worked in a-- when the Germans came in, she worked in a shop near-- in Warsaw, in a German shop.
But they took her away from the shop in Yom Kippur.
Was a selection in Warsaw, and I never heard of her.
My brother was a younger guy, Herschel.
He was born two years younger than my sister.
1917, yeah.
Was a very good looking boy, beautiful boy, tall for his age.
And when we were in Warsaw in the ghetto, he was still in the ghetto with us.
Later, when it was the-- the selection, and later the uprising, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had a letter that he is in Lvov, working as a mechanic in a German shop, with the cars.
And he was all right, and later was no news, any news.
It seems like when the war broke out with Russia, and the Germans start with the Russian war-- war, we didn't have any more news from him.
He got killed in that time when the Germans and the Russians was fighting.
He got someplace, he got lost.
Oh yes, I met my wife when I was 17 years old.
Through a cousin of mine, which was killed later in the war.
But she was a friend of my cousin, and I meet her this way.
They went to-- to school together or something.
I don't remember.
All I know is we met.
She was 15 years old, and I was 17.
And we got married a couple years later.
Eula, ?] and in English you say Julia.
Wildman, Wildman.
W-I-L-D-M-A-N.
Her birthday, December 8, 1914.
Well, when I met her she was still in school.
And she has no occupation really.
June 6-- July 6-- or June-- when was it?
July 6, right.
It was a quiet wedding in the rabbi's offices.
And no big deal.
Well, I was a printer.
And we had a printing shop.
I-- on [?
Pawia ?] 31 in Warsaw.
Had a couple people working for me.
And this way, you make a living.
No-- yes.
My daughter was born in August 6, I think, or was it September?
August or September.
Wait a second.
Let me think.
October 6.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] October.
No.
I-- when the war started, in September 1, they said on the radio that the Germans will kill all the Jews-- I mean, and then, on September 6, I left Warsaw with my brother-in-law.
My brother-in-law-- her brother-in-law, my-- I know how you say it.
And we left both for-- in direction of Russia.
And we spent the 10 weeks with the Russians, on the Russian side of Poland, which Poland was annexed by-- the-- this part of Poland was annexed by the Russians.
And we spent the 10 weeks.
Then I heard that my wife give-- gave birth to the baby.
And we both came back to Warsaw after 10 weeks being with the Russian side.
With the Russian [INAUDIBLE].
And matter of fact, we lived in a hotel, which was owned by my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] family.
And then, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we came back to Warsaw after 10 weeks.
Of course, and this hotel was taken by the Russian general staff, military staff.
And I had to make acquainted with a Jewish-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was not a general.
It was a-- I don't know his rank, and he told me I shouldn't go to Warsaw because we are going to Warsaw, anyway, so you come with the army.
But I didn't listen to him, and I went to Warsaw.
We have a very nice family life.
I was working and have a good business.
And we were young people, and all we do is stay with the family.
We live together with her parents in the same apartment that time.
As a matter of fact, the printing was in the same place.
As on the front it was a store, a front store on the street-- from the street.
And in the back we had the big apartment.
My pre-war address?
I mean--
--the printing?
Oh, [?
Pawia 31. ?]
No, not really.
Rather traditional because my father and his brother had to work Saturdays.
He worked in a big factory, which and mostly Polish people worked there.
He was a carpenter, and has to work Saturdays there.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Later on, he was on his own, and he built furniture.
Well, I was playing soccer, was my hobby.
There is another thing.
I was later on, when I was 13 years old, I finished school.
And I went to work.
And they work was I became an apprentice in the printing.
And this where I learned my trade.
As a matter of fact, I became a member of the union, printers union, which was dominated by the Jewish Bund, the Socialist Party, the Jewish Socialist Party.
I became a member of the youth section, which was at Tuchow.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] At the same time, I was a member in a Jewish choir in the big Warsaw synagogue, which I sang in this choir from 19-- from 13 years old until I was 17, as a soprano.
Yes.
We sang with [INAUDIBLE] and-- was the other cantors.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I forgot the other names too.
Koussevitzky, Moshe Koussevitzky.
[INAUDIBLE].
We usually spoke my home Jewish and Polish.
Hmm?
Whatever the need was for.
No, I went to a Jewish school.
So we have only Jewish children.
And we-- the school was taught in Hebrew, mostly Hebrew.
Translated Hebrew and Jewish.
No, we lived in the Jewish section, so I didn't have too much connection with the Polish population.
Very simple.
I had the school from morning to afternoon.
And I have some homework, a very hard-working school.
I mean, we-- we learned a lot.
The school was from the Jewish community school.
So only boys were there, not girls.
It was more like a religious school.
I think I was a good kid.
As I started, after 13 years old, after my bar mitzvah, as I said, I became a apprentice in a printing shop And I learned my trade there.
I was a printer from that time on, always be a printer, always was a printer.
Oh, it was all different parties, different kinds of Jews.
And it was very interesting, was a active Jewish community in Warsaw.
Political and organizational activities.
As I said, we didn't have too much contact with the non-Jews.
But in-- was normal relationships.
Not till the war.
Of course, we heard about what's going on in Germany.
That the Jews were expelled from Germany, Polish Jews were expelled from Germany.
And we know that something was gonna happen at the end, but we wouldn't expect a war.
In the last year before the war.
No, as a matter of fact, a couple of friends of mine left for Israel at that time by illegal immigration.
But having a printing shop on my own at that time, and everything was going right, we didn't-- we didn't plan to leave Warsaw.
All the news from the press, what was going on, but we couldn't do much to do anything about.
Of course the Zionist organization say we should go, everybody, to Israel.
But as you know, the borders are-- were closed to Israel.
The British didn't let in no Jews to Israel.
You had to smuggle-- smuggled to come in there.
And as long as you have a good house, a good business, it's not easy to leave everything and family to go to Israel.
As I said, I was a member of the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE], the Bund, which says it really didn't.
Now, later I was working with [INAUDIBLE], the Zionist organization.
And they-- they tried to send people to Israel.
So I didn't move.
I was still in Warsaw.
No.
No.
No pogroms.
Maybe some places in the, let's say-- in some places maybe there were some actions against the Jews, single Jews.
But was not pogroms.
The Jews wouldn't stand for it.
I remember the beginning of the war.
And then, we left Warsaw.
We heard airplanes flying.
And just that time was the beginning of bombardment of Warsaw.
But this was just the beginning.
And then, we stayed there for a couple days.
And we left Warsaw on the way to Russia.
We were bombarded on the way, on the road.
Some Russian airplanes came down, and a lot of people were on the road walking.
But we came to-- to-- away from Warsaw.
And that time was no Germans there.
No, no hiding, but we remember that-- I remember that in the beginning of the war, her father, my wife's father, was working with the-- with the rice import of China.
And he-- he arranged that a big package of rice [? both-- ?] for both our house.
Because we knew, in the beginning, maybe there no food to be get.
So we had rice, and we had sugar in the house.
So we had a little prepared for-- for the war time.
No.
In the very beginning, we have no anti-Jewish action.
First of all, I was not in Warsaw for the first 10 weeks.
But after I came back, we still have like separation between the Jews and the Polish population.
Even before the ghetto was opened, a special ghetto for Jews, but it was no change to the Germans made a ghetto for the Jews.
And the Jews who lived outside the Jewish borders have to move in-- into the ghetto.
Then a little who troubled was the Polish people moved in in the Jewish places.
And they were separate at that time.
A lot of people were glad of it.
It got rid of the Jewish and better buildings.
Because they took over the buildings which the Jews owned outside the Jewish section of Warsaw.
